what skipped sda?
when the system dolled out devices initially
your raid devices are configured as /dev/sg*
the 500GB drive probably raced the raid array to /dev/sda
and the 500GB won :)
interesting
if you noticed, everything else, including the drive that was on the same controller as sda, got a block device after the 500GB
the raid devices were initialized before ata devices
hmm... so my raid devices got bumped yet were still built?
they were just given different ata block device names. the software raid configured them based on controller location
even though when I built the raid I gave it block device names?
didn't md assign sg* block device names to it?
maybe... so when I tell md to use sda/b/c it then renames them to sga/b/c and works with them from then on?
internally, they are addressed similar to this : hd(0,0,0)
channel, device, partition
so, block names are irrelevant.
right... so I am the only one using the block device references when making the raid... internally the raid software is using the real ID's for it's job?
correct
ok, that makes more sense... if I had realzied that then I would have looked at the block device names differently than I did... one more lesson learned

two things - you can either remove the PPA and get back to original state, or you can install kernel - but you need said hardware support for amdgpu-pro. AFAIK its currently even only available for rx 4xx, with planned expansion towards 7xxx.
so if you go path 2, then you will end up with newer "radeon" driver.

you will be using NEWER "radeon" driver on your hardware, not "amdgpu-pro".
SO it's up to you final decision

76.2: Hey guys, I have set a passphrase for my RSA private key. How am I still able to view it using only "less" in the terminal without it prompting for the passphrase?
um... i assume you mean `less RSAPrivKeyFile` ?
because that's 'encrypted' - to actually USE the key you have to decrypt
and then you need the password
<teward> No, I mean the id_rsa file which contains the private key.
Can't someone just copy paste they key and use it?
same concept RSAPrivKeyFile is a placeholder not the real filename
do you see an Proc-Type: field in there, and it says ENCRYPTED ?
in the output
Teward, yeah it says encrypted.
then it won't help them if they don't have the password.
By the way, how do I respond to you directly? Which command do I use? Sorry, newbie here.
if there were no password on the key, i'd be worried.
just put my nick at the beginning like i'm doing.
ike this
<teward> Not sure if it's working.
yep, that works and pings me :)
it's working.
h, cool : )
the password prompt is when it's encrypted, as long as you have a secure password you're relatively OK
eah, I'm a little confused about that. Let me briefly describe what's going through my mind, and maybe you could clear some of the misconceptions I might have.
So, I create two RSA keys, one public and one private. I stored the public key on my server, and now I set a password on my private key.
sure, but consider i'm also still at work, slow response times are important
aha, sure. Sorry to disturb you at work.
ping me at the beginning of each line too so i don't miss anything

y question is that why can I still view my id_rsa file containing my private key using just the "less" command, and it doesn't even ask for my password before I view it using "less." If someone were somehow able to get access to my account, couldn't they just copy the key and store it in another file, then use it to access my server?
because they still need your password to use it
mean, when I encrypt the file using, say filevault on OS X, it would prompt me for the password before I can view the contents. Here, it doesn't, that's why I'm a little confused.
see.
that's a different 'encryption' - likely it's being encrypted with a different key, and that needs a password first so you can gain access to the already-encrypted private key
when you generate a key and it asks you for the password, that's the password to decrypt the key for use when you need to verify ownership of the key

to that end, having the viewability of the private key, while it's encrypted with a password and they don't know the password you put on it, they can't really do anything with it without bruteforcing the pw
see, so if I copy pasted the key to another file (say id_rsa_copy), and used it on another computer, the file won't be the same as the original id_rsa file?
lets put it in a different way that may be more understandable.
aha, okay cool. Definitely.
i have a file.  I say "This is the public information.  The secure, top secret information is in this here safe.".  The safe in question has a pincode that is unknown digits long.
them having the public information and the safe doesn't help them if they want the secret information
insomuch that because they need my pincode to open said safe, or they have to brute force open the safe.

the same concept applies here: they could have your Public Key, and your Private Key, but since your Private Key is encrypted, they either need to brute force the password you set on it, or they need the password, to actually authenticate as the private key
because it's encrypted simply having the encrypted private key doesn't help them since they can't put the password that would 'decrypt' the encrypted private key for use

the nearest analogy is that "private info in the safe vs. public disclosed info on the same thing" example I just gave you - they don't know the pincode to the safe so they can't get to the private information
similarly, without your password or brute forcing the password, your encrypted private key is of no real use to them
but your actual concern shouldn't be "What if they get my key", your concern at that point is "If they have my encrypted private key they're in my system."
and the moment they're in your system, you are quite literally screwed.
so I'd be less worried about your private key which is encrypted.  And more worried about the security of the machine HOLDING the private key
(the safest thing is to keep the private keys off of your computer on encrypted media)
Ah, I see. So, there's another "layer" of security in the file, and just being able to view the file and see the encryption key doesn't allow them to actually "use" they key?
h, I see. So, there's another "layer" of security in the file, and just being able to view the file and see the encryption key doesn't allow them to actually "use" they key?
hoops, forgot to mention you first time : )
i saw what you were saying since i'm watching the channel right now.
ut if I kept the keys off of my computer, then I wouldn't be able to access my server right?

Another example that's even more laymans-terms than the one i gave is this: I give you a list of names, and a locked box. I don't give you the key.  I say "The personal information tied to each of these names is in the locked box"
so you can't get to that personal information without the key to the box.  What you see when you `less` your private key is the key after it's encrypted
the decryption 'key' isn't stored in there, afaik, and the 'decryption' of the key won't work without the password
and if you keep the keys off your computer, on an encrypted drive, you can specify the key file to use with `-i /path/to/file`
without backticks but meh
which is what I do, but I"m insane and paranoid so meh
so your ultimate concern is not "They have my encrypted private key!" but "Did I use a strong passphrase on the key" and "Do they know my password"

sorry

you're seeing the "encrypted" key, not the actual key, yes.
i.e. the safe, or the locked box, in my laymans-terms examples

just make sure to use strong passwords that're different than your logon passwords and such, to make it that much harder for them to guess the passwords
sure will! : )

and always remember: if you're afraid about the security of the private key on your computer, you should actually be worried about them gaining access to that computer with the keys on it rather than the keys, since the bigger risk is introduced by them popping your system

in 0700 what is 4= ? 2= ? 1=? again.

4=read, 2=write, 1=execute,  <username> is the name of each user you want write permission to
oh i get the username already... i'm adding w to their /home/<username>
yeah, you're just making it so that the group has write access (although if each user is in the same group then all users can read/write to each others home)
chmod g+w adds write access (+w) to the groups (g) they belong to?
exactly
ah yeah... but then they'll also be able to write in my /home
not if you set your home to 700 (read/write/execute for the owner only)
ah that's the permissions part of it...
yeah, "sudo chmod 700 ~/" is the same as it being "drwx------"
so if i want them to read my files only, i set chmod 0750? not including others
i'll try to get this straight...
yeah, that will let them read files in you're home (but remember that each file and directory under your home can have different permissions too)
so you can have directories and files they can't read even though your home is set to be readable

so that /home/folder is 0777, but /home/folder/file is 0000.... which will take effect?
if the directory is set readable to all but a file inside it is set to readable only to an owner (user), then the files permissions take president
so file permissions are always above permissions for directory?
well, in a way. you see to linux, directories are "files" too ;)
ah yes... i read that a thousand times already... :)
you have to have access to the directory to access what's inside it, but you can set different permissions on files inside them

that's exactly how it works :)
gvsa123 is trying to absorb :)

for example, if you suid (set UID) an executable, then when a user runs that executable it's ran as if the owner ran it (rather than the user)
User ID?
yeah, same for sgid (set Group ID), it'd be ran with the permissions of the group that owns the file
have a look at the permissions for /usr/bin/X, the "s" in there represents suid and sgid (depending where it is in the permissions)
but you'd have to have x permission to run it in the first place?
you do, yes :)

"-rwsr-sr-x 1 root root 7460 2007-12-20 19:40 /usr/bin/X" means that anyone can run it, but when they do it'll run as if the user and group that ran it was "root"

yes
why root? and it still applies to the others group when it doesn't have an s?
oh because it is owned and belongs to root
if it was only -rwsr-xr-x, then it would be ran as user: root, group: (your group)
the other permission "sticky bit" is rather obscure and not really used any more, so you don't have to worry about it

when it says "2.14.2-0ubuntu2" is marked for installation, that has to be because something else is marked for installation and 2.4.2-0ubuntu2 is a dependency
